prs.title: fix: Fix formatDistanceStrict rounding inaccuracy
prs.body:
## Description

This PR fixes an issue where `formatDistanceStrict` would return incorrect values for `year` and `month` units due to naive division using fixed constants (e.g., assuming every year is exactly `minutesInYear`).

For example, `20 years - 5 days` was previously calculated as `20 years` even when `roundingMethod: 'floor'` was used, because the actual duration in minutes (including leap years) was slightly larger than `20 * minutesInYear`.

### Changes

- **`formatDistanceStrict`**: Updated to use `differenceInYears` and `differenceInMonths` for accurate calendar-based calculation.
- **Rounding**: Implemented precise rounding logic by calculating the remainder using `addYears`/`addMonths`.
- **Dependencies**: Updated `.gitmodules` to use HTTPS URLs instead of SSH. This fixes `pnpm install` failures in environments where SSH keys are not set up (e.g., CI or some local setups).

## Verification

- Added verification cases confirming:
    - `20 years - 5 days` with `floor` now correctly returns **19 years**.
    - `5 months - 2 days` with `floor` now correctly returns **4 months**.
    - Exact durations are still correct.
- Ran existing tests: `npm run test src/formatDistanceStrict/test.ts` passed.
- Verified dependency installation works with `pnpm install`.

## Related Issue

Fixes #4074

prs.title: feat: add transformerNotationFold
prs.body:
## Description

This PR adds a new transformer `transformerNotationFold` to `@shikijs/transformers`. This transformer allows users to mark sections of code as foldable using comment notation, similar to VS Code or Monaco Editor.

It supports two syntaxes:
1. **Shiki-style**: `// [!code fold:start]` and `// [!code fold:end]`
2. **Region-style**: `// #region` and `// #endregion`

## Changes

- Added `packages/transformers/src/transformers/notation-fold.ts`
- Exported `transformerNotationFold` in `packages/transformers/src/index.ts`
- Added tests in `packages/transformers/test/fold.test.ts`

## Usage

### Shiki Syntax

```javascript
// [!code fold:start] variables
let a = 1
// [!code fold:end]
```

### Region Syntax

```javascript
// #region variables
let a = 1
// #endregion
```

## Implementation Details

The transformer adds the following classes to the generated HTML:
- `fold-start`: Added to the line containing the start marker (for `#region`) or the line following the start marker (for `[!code fold:start]`).
- `fold-end`: Added to the line containing the end marker (for `#endregion`) or the line preceding the end marker (for `[!code fold:end]`).
- `fold-content`: Added to all lines between the start and end lines.

Note: Shiki-style markers (`[!code fold:...]`) are removed from the output, while `#region` markers are preserved (but still get the classes).

## Verification

Added a new test file `packages/transformers/test/fold.test.ts` which verifies:
- Correct class application for both syntaxes.
- Nested folding support (via stack implementation).
- Correct handling of line removal for Shiki markers.


closes: #861

prs.title: Fix issue 861
prs.body:
# Feat: Add `transformerNotationFold`

## Description

This PR adds a new transformer `transformerNotationFold` to `@shikijs/transformers`. This transformer allows users to mark sections of code as foldable using comment notation, similar to VS Code or Monaco Editor.

It supports two syntaxes:
1. **Shiki-style**: `// [!code fold:start]` and `// [!code fold:end]`
2. **Region-style**: `// #region` and `// #endregion`

## Changes

- Added `packages/transformers/src/transformers/notation-fold.ts`
- Exported `transformerNotationFold` in `packages/transformers/src/index.ts`
- Added tests in `packages/transformers/test/fold.test.ts`

## Usage

### Shiki Syntax

```javascript
// [!code fold:start] variables
let a = 1
// [!code fold:end]
```

### Region Syntax

```javascript
// #region variables
let a = 1
// #endregion
```

## Implementation Details

The transformer adds the following classes to the generated HTML:
- `fold-start`: Added to the line containing the start marker (for `#region`) or the line following the start marker (for `[!code fold:start]`).
- `fold-end`: Added to the line containing the end marker (for `#endregion`) or the line preceding the end marker (for `[!code fold:end]`).
- `fold-content`: Added to all lines between the start and end lines.

Note: Shiki-style markers (`[!code fold:...]`) are removed from the output, while `#region` markers are preserved (but still get the classes).

## Verification

Added a new test file `packages/transformers/test/fold.test.ts` which verifies:
- Correct class application for both syntaxes.
- Nested folding support (via stack implementation).
- Correct handling of line removal for Shiki markers.

closes: #861

prs.title: fix: reorder builtin decoration transformers
prs.body:
# Fix: `metaHighlightWord` fails with `twoslash`

## Description

Fixes an issue where `metaHighlightWord` (and other decorations) would throw "Invalid decoration offset" errors when used in conjunction with `twoslash`.

## Changes

### `packages/core`

- Moved `builtInTransformers` (which includes [transformerDecorations](file:///Users/amanjeet/Desktop/GAME/shiki/packages/core/src/transformer-decorations.ts#21-194)) to run *before* other transformers in [getTransformers](file:///Users/amanjeet/Desktop/GAME/shiki/packages/core/src/highlight/_get-transformers.ts#8-17).
  - **Reason**: `transformerTwoslash` modifies the HAST structure (e.g., adding error lines, popups). If [transformerDecorations](file:///Users/amanjeet/Desktop/GAME/shiki/packages/core/src/transformer-decorations.ts#21-194) runs after `transformerTwoslash`, it attempts to apply decorations based on the original code offsets to a modified HAST, causing mismatches. Running decorations first ensures they are applied to the correct structure.

### `packages/twoslash`

- Explicitly cleared `decorations` in the recursive [codeToHast](file:///Users/amanjeet/Desktop/GAME/shiki/packages/core/src/highlight/code-to-hast.ts#23-85) call within [rendererRich](file:///Users/amanjeet/Desktop/GAME/shiki/packages/twoslash/src/renderer-rich.ts#204-708).
  - **Reason**: When rendering popups, [rendererRich](file:///Users/amanjeet/Desktop/GAME/shiki/packages/twoslash/src/renderer-rich.ts#204-708) calls [codeToHast](file:///Users/amanjeet/Desktop/GAME/shiki/packages/core/src/highlight/code-to-hast.ts#23-85) for the popup content. It was inheriting `decorations` from the parent context. Since the popup content is different from the main code, applying these decorations caused invalid offset errors.

## Reproduction

```ts
import { codeToHtml } from 'shiki'
import { transformerTwoslash } from '@shikijs/twoslash'
import { transformerMetaWordHighlight } from '@shikijs/transformers'

const code = 'const a = 1'
const html = await codeToHtml(code, {
  lang: 'javascript',
  theme: 'vitesse-dark',
  transformers: [
    transformerMetaWordHighlight(),
    transformerTwoslash({ explicitTrigger: true })
  ],
  meta: {
    __raw: '/codeToHtml/ twoslash'
  }
})
```

## Checklist

- [x] Tests passed
- [x] Linting passed

closes: #990

prs.title: fix(adepter-pg): normalize MONEY values for Decimal
prs.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes the parsing of PostgreSQL `MONEY` values in `@prisma/adapter-pg`.  
Currently, `normalize_money` simply uses `money.slice(1)`, which only removes the
leading currency symbol and does not handle thousands separators, locale formats,
or values without a symbol. This causes Prisma to pass invalid strings such as
`"50,000.00"` to `Decimal`, resulting in:

`[DecimalError] Invalid argument: 50,000.00`

This behavior worked in Prisma 6 and regressed in Prisma 7.

## Changes

- Updated `normalize_money` to:
  - Strip currency symbols, spaces, and letters
  - Remove thousands separators
  - Detect locale decimal separators
  - Normalize all MONEY strings into valid `Decimal`-compatible numeric strings
- Supports both US-style (`$50,000.00`) and EU-style (`€1.234,56`) formats.

## Reproduction

Using PostgreSQL with a `MONEY` column:

```prisma
model Order {
  id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  price     Decimal  @db.Money
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
}
````

Aggregating:

```ts
await prisma.order.aggregate({
  _sum: { price: true }
});
```

### Before (Prisma 7.0.0)

Throws:
`[DecimalError] Invalid argument: 50,000.00`

### After (this PR)

Returns the correct aggregated decimal value with no errors.
closes: #28674

## Regression

This issue did not exist in Prisma 6.x.
The regression appears after introduction of the new adapter conversion pipeline in Prisma 7.

## Testing

Verified locally using the PostgreSQL sandbox with MONEY values inserted such as:

* `$50,000.00`
* `€1.234,56`

Aggregate queries now succeed without errors and return correct numeric results.
closes: #28674

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **Bug Fixes**
  * Improved PostgreSQL money type handling to correctly parse US/EU formats, currency symbols, negative representations (including parentheses), ambiguous separators, leading zeros and other edge cases.

* **Tests**
  * Added extensive unit and functional tests covering normalization, parsing, aggregations, raw queries and many edge-case scenarios.

* **Documentation**
  * Added a manual test guide detailing end-to-end verification steps for money-type behavior.

<sub>✏️ Tip: You can customize this high-level summary in your review settings.</sub>
<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

prs.title: fix(adepter-pg): normalize MONEY values for Decimal
prs.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes the parsing of PostgreSQL `MONEY` values in `@prisma/adapter-pg`.  
Currently, `normalize_money` simply uses `money.slice(1)`, which only removes the
leading currency symbol and does not handle thousands separators, locale formats,
or values without a symbol. This causes Prisma to pass invalid strings such as
`"50,000.00"` to `Decimal`, resulting in:

`[DecimalError] Invalid argument: 50,000.00`

This behavior worked in Prisma 6 and regressed in Prisma 7.

## Changes

- Updated `normalize_money` to:
  - Strip currency symbols, spaces, and letters
  - Remove thousands separators
  - Detect locale decimal separators
  - Normalize all MONEY strings into valid `Decimal`-compatible numeric strings
- Supports both US-style (`$50,000.00`) and EU-style (`€1.234,56`) formats.

## Reproduction

Using PostgreSQL with a `MONEY` column:

```prisma
model Order {
  id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  price     Decimal  @db.Money
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
}
````

Aggregating:

```ts
await prisma.order.aggregate({
  _sum: { price: true }
});
```

### Before (Prisma 7.0.0)

Throws:
`[DecimalError] Invalid argument: 50,000.00`

### After (this PR)

Returns the correct aggregated decimal value with no errors.

## Regression

This issue did not exist in Prisma 6.x.
The regression appears after introduction of the new adapter conversion pipeline in Prisma 7.

## Testing

Verified locally using the PostgreSQL sandbox with MONEY values inserted such as:

* `$50,000.00`
* `€1.234,56`

Aggregate queries now succeed without errors and return correct numeric results.

prs.title: fix(dropdown): allow anchor navigation for real href links
prs.body:
This PR fixes the issue where clicking a dropdown item containing a normal
`<a href="...">` link does not navigate to its target page.

Materialize’s dropdown click logic currently prevents the default click behavior
for all items, which unintentionally blocks navigation for legitimate links.

This change adds a safe early-return in `_handleDropdownClick()`:

* If the clicked element is an `<a>`
* **AND** the `href` is not a hash link (`#something`)
* **AND** not a `javascript:` pseudo-protocol

…then the browser is allowed to perform normal navigation.

This preserves all existing behavior for:

* `href="#"` (in-page navigation)
* `href="javascript:void(0)"` (JS-only actions)
* `onItemClick` callbacks
* keyboard-based selection behavior

Fixes: **#6683**

## Types of changes

* [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
* [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
* [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to change)

---

## Checklist

* [x] I have read the **CONTRIBUTING** document.
* [ ] My change requires documentation updates.
* [ ] I have updated the documentation accordingly.
* [ ] I have added tests to cover my changes. *(Not required for this UI fix.)*
* [x] All existing tests pass (build completed successfully).

---

prs.title: Add custom 404 page and proper error handling
prs.body:
## Summary

This pull request addresses issue #89, "Improve 404 Error Handling by Adding a Custom 404 Page."  It implements a dedicated 404 page to provide a better user experience when users navigate to non-existent routes. The server now also responds with a correct 404 HTTP status code.

## Changes

*   **Created `src/views/404.handlebars`:** Added a new Handlebars view to display a user-friendly "Page Not Found" message.  The page includes a clear error message and a link back to the homepage.
*   **Updated `src/index.js`:** Modified the Express app's catch-all route handler to render the `404.handlebars` view instead of redirecting to the homepage. The route handler now explicitly sets the HTTP status code to 404 using `res.status(404)`.
*   **Improved Project Structure:** Ensured proper error handling and separated concerns for better maintainability.

## Details

The previous behavior of redirecting to the homepage was confusing for users and negatively impacted SEO. This change provides a more intuitive error experience.  By responding with the correct HTTP status code, the application also now aligns with web standards, which is important for API consumers.

## Testing

1.  Navigate to a non-existent route in the application (e.g., `/this-page-does-not-exist`).
2.  Verify that the `404.handlebars` view is displayed.
3.  Inspect the HTTP response status code using your browser's developer tools or a tool like `curl`. The status code should be 404.

## Related Issue

Fixes #89: "Improve 404 Error Handling by Adding a Custom 404 Page"

## Checklist

*   [x] A `404.handlebars` file exists in `src/views` and provides a clear error message.
*   [x] The Express app renders `404.handlebars` instead of redirecting to `/`.
*   [x] The server correctly responds with a 404 status code for non-existent routes.

## Additional Notes

CSS styling for this 404 is kept to the minimum to match existing pages. A maintainer may want to improve visual appeal.

issues.title: Improve 404 Error Handling by Adding a Custom 404 Page
issues.body:
## Summary

Currently, when a user navigates to a non-existent route in the application, they are redirected to the home page (`/`). This is confusing and doesn't provide proper error handling. This issue proposes implementing a custom 404 page with the correct HTTP status code.

## Expected Behavior

*   Users should see a dedicated "404 - Page Not Found" message when visiting an invalid URL.
*   The server should return a 404 HTTP status code.
*   The design of the 404 page should be consistent with the application's style.

## Current Behavior

*   Any unknown route redirects to the home page (`/`).
*   No proper 404 error handling exists.
*   The HTTP status code for missing pages is incorrect.

## Proposed Solution

1.  Create a `404.handlebars` view in the `views/` directory with a user-friendly message and a "Go back home" button.
2.  Update the Express app (likely in `src/index.js`) to render this view when an unknown route is requested.
3.  Ensure the server responds with a 404 status code when serving the 404 page.  This may involve using `res.status(404)` in the route handler.

## Acceptance Criteria

*   [ ] A `404.handlebars` file exists in `src/views` and provides a clear error message.
*   [ ] The Express app renders `404.handlebars` instead of redirecting to `/`.
*   [ ] The server correctly responds with a 404 status code for non-existent routes.

## Additional Context

This enhancement will improve user experience, SEO, and align the application with web standards for handling missing pages.  It would also benefit API consumers who rely on correct status codes for error handling.

## Relevant Code

The primary file to modify is likely `src/index.js` where routing is handled.
